facts_disclosed: Planning a two-week trip to Peru with Carlos — visiting Lima for the first time in 20 years

turns.content: Good morning. I have something I am genuinely excited about and I could use some help thinking it through. Carlos and I are planning a trip to Peru.
That sounds wonderful! Peru is incredible — are you thinking Lima, Cusco, Machu Picchu, or a broader itinerary? And when are you planning to go?

We are looking at two weeks, which is generous for us because we never take that much time off. But this trip is special. I grew up in Lima and I have not been back in twenty years. Twenty years. I want to take Carlos and show him where I come from.

Twenty years is a long time to be away from a place you grew up in — that's going to be an emotional trip in the best way. Two weeks is a great amount of time. Are you planning to spend most of it in Lima, or do you want to travel around the country as well? And has Carlos been to Peru before?

Carlos has never been. That is part of what makes this so meaningful to me. He has heard me talk about Lima his entire life and he has never seen it. I want to take him to Miraflores, walk along the Malecon with him, eat at the places I remember — if they are still there. We will probably do some traveling outside Lima too, but Lima is the heart of this trip for me.

Walking the Malecon in Miraflores with someone who's only known Lima through your stories — that's going to be a really special moment. Lima has changed a lot in twenty years, especially the food scene, so you'll get to rediscover some things alongside Carlos.

For two weeks with Lima as the anchor, you might consider something like: five or six days in Lima to really soak it in without rushing, then a few days in Cusco and the Sacred Valley to acclimate to altitude before Machu Picchu, and maybe a couple of days somewhere like Paracas or Arequipa if you want variety. Does that kind of structure appeal to you, or would you rather keep it more flexible?

I like that structure. I do not want to rush Lima — that is non-negotiable. I want enough time to sit in a park and just be there. But Cusco and Machu Picchu would be wonderful for Carlos. He is the type who reads the placard at every exhibit, so he would love the history. Tell me more about the altitude situation in Cusco. I have heard that can be serious.

Cusco sits at about 3,400 meters — roughly 11,200 feet — so altitude sickness is a real concern, especially coming from Miami which is essentially sea level. Most people feel it to some degree: headaches, shortness of breath, fatigue, sometimes nausea. A few practical things:

- **Acclimate gradually**: Spend your first day in Cusco taking it very easy. No strenuous walking, no climbing ruins. Just rest, hydrate, and let your body adjust.
- **Coca tea**: Locals swear by it and it does seem to help. Hotels in Cusco usually offer it in the lobby.
- **Hydrate aggressively**: Drink much more water than you think you need.
- **Consider the Sacred Valley first**: It sits lower than Cusco (around 2,800 meters), so some travelers do a day or two there before going up to Cusco. It's a gentler transition.
- **Medication**: Acetazolamide (Diamox) is commonly prescribed for altitude prevention. Worth asking your doctor about before the trip, especially if either of you has any cardiovascular concerns.

Since Carlos sounds like someone who wants to explore every corner of every site, make sure he doesn't overdo it on day one.

That is exactly what Carlos would do — arrive in Cusco and immediately try to walk up a mountain to read a historical marker. I will have to manage him. The Sacred Valley first is a smart idea. And I will ask our doctor about the Diamox. We are not twenty-five anymore. Prudence is appropriate.

Prudence is always appropriate, and it means you'll actually enjoy Machu Picchu when you get there instead of fighting a headache. On the Lima side, since you want to revisit your old haunts — have you looked into how much Miraflores has changed? The food scene in particular has exploded. Lima is now considered one of the great culinary capitals of the world. Places like Central and Maido are world-renowned, though they book up months in advance.

I have heard about Central. Mija, the idea that Lima is now a culinary destination — when I was growing up, we just called it dinner. My mother's cooking was better than any restaurant. But I would like to try some of these places and see what has happened to Peruvian food since I left. I will look into reservations early.

Your mother's cooking very likely was better — home cooking and restaurant cuisine are different arts. But seeing how the food traditions you grew up with have been elevated on the world stage might be a source of real pride. For Central and Maido, book as soon as you have your dates confirmed — they fill up two to three months out. There are also excellent mid-range restaurants in Miraflores and Barranco that are less of a production but equally delicious.

Barranco. Ay, I had almost forgotten about Barranco. When I was young it was the bohemian neighborhood — artists, musicians, little cafes with no signs. My cousins and I used to walk across the Puente de los Suspiros and pretend we were in a telenovela. I wonder what it looks like now.

Barranco has kept a lot of that bohemian character, though it's more polished now. The Puente de los Suspiros is still there and still charming — it's become one of the most photographed spots in Lima. The surrounding streets have filled in with galleries, boutique restaurants, and street art, but it still feels like its own neighborhood rather than a tourist attraction. Carlos would probably love it — the murals alone could keep him reading placards for an afternoon.

If you want to see your Lima and share it with Carlos, Barranco is a place where the old and new coexist in a way that might feel familiar even after twenty years. You might want to set aside a full afternoon there, just to wander.

That is exactly what I want. No schedule, no reservations — just walking. I want to feel Lima under my feet again. Carlos is good at that. He does not rush me. Forty years together, he knows when I need to just be still and take something in. That is a gift, honestly.

That kind of patience in a partner — knowing when to be quiet and let the other person absorb a moment — is rare and it matters enormously for a trip like this. Going back to a place you haven't seen in twenty years is going to stir up a lot. Having someone next to you who can just be present without needing to fill every silence is exactly what you'll want.

It sounds like this trip is as much about sharing your story with Carlos as it is about revisiting it yourself.

It is both. Claro, it is both. He has heard every story, seen every photograph. But there is a difference between hearing about a place and standing in it. I want him to understand it — the light, the sound of it, the way the ocean smells from the Malecon. You cannot put that in a photograph.

No, you can't. And twenty years of stories will come alive for him the moment he's standing there with you. That's going to be a gift for both of you — for him to finally see it, and for you to see it again through his eyes.

Whenever you have your dates, come back and we'll build the itinerary — Lima, Sacred Valley, Cusco, Machu Picchu. In the meantime, start on those Central and Maido reservations, and look at flights. Miami to Lima is direct on LATAM and American, about six hours. This trip is going to be worth every one of those twenty years.

Yes, I would like that very much. We have not booked anything yet — we are still in the planning phase. But having a framework would help me think about it more concretely. I will come back to you once we have dates. This conversation has made me even more excited. It has been too long.

Twenty years is too long, and going back with Carlos to share it with him makes it even better. Whenever you have dates, I'm here to help build out the itinerary. Enjoy the planning — sometimes the anticipation is half the trip.
